content.text: War Room Tooling and Collaboration Standards

The war room is wherever the response team assembles to resolve an incident—virtual in most modern organizations, but still a defined space with defined rules. Tooling that is unfamiliar, access that requires a ticket, and norms that differ by team are all forms of friction that extend time-to-mitigate. War room standards eliminate that friction before the incident occurs so the team can focus entirely on resolution.
The Tool Stack

attrs.alt: War room tooling stack for agentic AI incident response

content.text: AI-Specific Tooling Requirements

The core distinction between agentic AI incident response and conventional software incident response is the need for AI-specific debugging tools. When an agent produces a wrong output, the root cause may be in the prompt, the model version, a retrieved context chunk, a tool response, or a combination of all four. No general-purpose log viewer surfaces this signal without customization.

Trace viewer with agent step visualization: must show the full agent loop—each LLM call, tool invocation, and intermediate thought—as a sequential timeline with token counts and latency per step. LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, and custom Jaeger dashboards with agent-step span attributes serve this purpose.

Eval score dashboard: a real-time view of output quality metrics broken down by model version, prompt version, and agent type. The IC must be able to see whether a quality drop correlates with a recent prompt deployment.

Prompt diff viewer: when a prompt version rollback or change is suspected as a root cause, the team needs a side-by-side diff of the current and previous prompt versions. This is often a simple internal tool that wraps the prompt registry.

Session replay: the ability to re-execute a specific user session against different model or prompt versions to reproduce the failure in a sandboxed environment, confirming the root cause before deploying a fix.
Slack Channel Standards

The incident Slack channel is the written record of the incident and the primary coordination surface for team members not on the bridge call. Consistent naming and usage conventions allow post-incident search and audit without manual effort.
Bridge Call Protocol

The bridge call is the synchronous coordination layer. It supplements Slack, not replaces it. Key decisions happen on the bridge call; all decisions are echoed to Slack by the scribe within 60 seconds.

IC starts the bridge call within 3 minutes of declaring the incident. A standard Zoom or Google Meet link is pre-configured per team and never changes, so team members can join without needing a link in the heat of the moment.
All participants mute when not speaking. The IC is the moderator and can mute participants who create background noise or speak over others.
The IC gives a 2-minute situation update at the start of every 5-minute cycle: current status, what changed since last update, what the team is working on, what is needed from whom.

Sidebar conversations about non-incident topics are prohibited during the bridge call. Technical rabbit holes that involve only 2 people should move to a Slack thread; the bridge call is for IC coordination, not technical debugging.
Screen sharing: the active debugger shares their terminal or Grafana dashboard. The IC shares the incident summary document. Both can be shared simultaneously using split screen.
Incident Timeline Document

The incident timeline document is a living Google Doc or Confluence page that the scribe maintains in real time. It serves as the authoritative record during the incident and becomes the foundation for the postmortem timeline afterward.

# Incident Timeline: inc-2026-07-14-003

## Header
- **Severity:** SEV-2 (escalated from SEV-3 at 09:15 UTC)
- **Category:** Infrastructure / Rate Limit Exhaustion
- **IC:** Alice Chen
- **Technical Lead:** Bob Kumar
- **Scribe:** Marcus Lee
- **Declared:** 2026-07-14 09:03 UTC
- **Resolved:** TBD

## Timeline

| Time (UTC) | Event | Actor | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 09:03 | PagerDuty alert: llm_429_rate > 5% | Automated | Incident declared |
| 09:05 | Incident channel #inc-2026-07-14-003 opened | Alice (IC) | Team assembled |
| 09:06 | Status page updated: Investigating | Alice (IC) | Users notified |
| 09:08 | Root cause identified: token quota exhausted | Bob (TL) | — |
| 09:10 | Severity upgraded SEV-3 → SEV-2 (15% user impact) | Alice (IC) | Exec notified |
| 09:15 | Fallback LLM provider traffic split initiated | Bob (TL) | Error rate falling |
| 09:22 | Error rate < 1%, monitoring for stability | Bob (TL) | — |
| 09:52 | 30-min stable window complete | Alice (IC) | All-clear declared |

## Key Decisions
- 09:10: Severity escalated to SEV-2 based on confirmed 15% user impact
- 09:15: Decision to split traffic 50/50 to fallback provider

## Outstanding Actions
- [ ] Root cause: why did batch job consume 40% of daily quota overnight?
- [ ] Preventive: add quota alerting at 60% daily usage threshold

## Notifications Sent
- Status page: 09:06, 09:15, 09:52
- Engineering director: 09:10 Slack DM
- Vendor TAM: 09:12 email (awaiting response)
Pre-Provisioned Tool Access

Access requests during an incident are a coordination tax that extends resolution time. Every tool in the war room stack must be pre-provisioned for all potential incident responders before any incident occurs. The following table defines minimum required access by role.
Tool
IC
Technical Lead
Scribe
Grafana (read)
Required
Required
Optional
Jaeger / Trace Viewer
Required
Required
Not required
Status page (edit)
Required
Not required
Required
PagerDuty (manage)
Required
Optional
Not required
Session replay tool
Optional
Required
Not required
Automating War Room Setup

Every minute spent creating a Slack channel, finding the Zoom link, and locating the runbook is a minute not spent on mitigation. The following Python script, triggered from PagerDuty webhooks, automates the war room setup so the IC arrives to a prepared environment.

"""war_room_setup.py -- automated war room bootstrap on incident declaration."""
import os
import httpx
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from classification_tree import Classification


SLACK_TOKEN = os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"]
JIRA_TOKEN = os.environ["JIRA_API_TOKEN"]
JIRA_BASE = os.environ["JIRA_BASE_URL"]
STATUSPAGE_API_KEY = os.environ["STATUSPAGE_API_KEY"]
STATUSPAGE_PAGE_ID = os.environ["STATUSPAGE_PAGE_ID"]

RUNBOOK_BASE = "https://wiki.internal/runbooks"
ZOOM_LINK = os.environ.get("INCIDENT_BRIDGE_ZOOM_LINK", "https://zoom.us/j/123456789")


def setup_war_room(cls: Classification, incident_id: str) -> dict:
    """Bootstrap the war room for a classified incident.
    
    Returns a dict with channel_id, jira_url, and statuspage_component_id.
    """
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    channel_name = f"inc-{now.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}-{incident_id[-3:]}"
    if cls.severity == "SEV-1":
        channel_name = "sev1-" + channel_name

    runbook_url = f"{RUNBOOK_BASE}/{cls.category}/{cls.severity.lower()}-{cls.category[:5]}.md"

    # Create Slack channel
    with httpx.Client() as client:
        resp = client.post(
            "https://slack.com/api/conversations.create",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SLACK_TOKEN}"},
            json={"name": channel_name, "is_private": cls.severity == "SEV-1"},
        )
        channel_id = resp.json()["channel"]["id"]

        # Post pinned summary
        summary = (
            f"*INCIDENT OPEN*\n"
            f"Severity: {cls.severity} | Category: {cls.category}\n"
            f"Owner team: {cls.owner_team}\n"
            f"Runbook: {runbook_url}\n"
            f"Bridge: {ZOOM_LINK}\n"
            f"Declared at: {now.isoformat()}"
        )
        msg_resp = client.post(
            "https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SLACK_TOKEN}"},
            json={"channel": channel_id, "text": summary},
        )
        client.post(
            "https://slack.com/api/pins.add",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {SLACK_TOKEN}"},
            json={"channel": channel_id, "timestamp": msg_resp.json()["ts"]},
        )

        # Create Jira incident ticket
        jira_resp = client.post(
            f"{JIRA_BASE}/rest/api/3/issue",
            headers={
                "Authorization": f"Bearer {JIRA_TOKEN}",
                "Content-Type": "application/json",
            },
            json={
                "fields": {
                    "project": {"key": "INC"},
                    "summary": f"[{cls.severity}][{cls.category}] Incident {incident_id}",
                    "issuetype": {"name": "Incident"},
                    "priority": {"name": "Critical" if cls.severity in ("SEV-1", "SEV-2") else "High"},
                    "description": {"type": "doc", "version": 1,
                                   "content": [{"type": "paragraph",
                                               "content": [{"type": "text", "text": summary}]}]},
                    "customfield_10100": cls.severity,  # custom: severity field
                    "customfield_10101": cls.category,  # custom: category field
                }
            },
        )
        jira_key = jira_resp.json()["key"]

    return {"channel_id": channel_id, "channel_name": channel_name, "jira_key": jira_key}
Collaboration Discipline During the War Room

Tooling alone does not produce effective collaboration. The war room must operate with explicit behavioral norms, especially because high-stress, sleep-deprived, time-pressured environments degrade communication quality. The following practices are enforced protocol, not suggestions.
Structured Communication in Chat
Thread everything.
Reply to specific messages in Slack threads rather than top-level. This keeps the channel scannable. The IC reads the channel top-level; SMEs work in threads.
Use status emoji.
React with :white_check_mark: when an action item is complete, :clock3: when in progress, and :x: when ruled out. The IC can scan the channel and get status without asking.
Tag the IC explicitly.

Any message that requires IC decision or acknowledgment starts with '@IC_name Decision needed:' or '@IC_name FYI:'. IC does not read every message; explicit tags ensure nothing decision-critical is missed.
Log commands before running.

Post the command to the channel before executing in production. Format: 'Running: `kubectl rollout restart deployment/llm-api -n prod` — reason: clear stuck pods'. This creates an audit trail and gives IC a chance to intervene.
Screen Share and Dashboard Discipline
Share links, not screens.
Post a direct URL to the Grafana panel or Datadog dashboard in the channel. Others can open it independently, zoom in, change time ranges, and interact.
Annotate screenshots with timestamps.
When posting a screenshot as evidence, annotate the time range and which metric you're highlighting. A raw screenshot of a noisy dashboard conveys little.
Share your screen only for walk-throughs.
Screen share is appropriate when an SME walks the IC through a complex sequence of findings. For static evidence, a link or annotated screenshot is always preferred.
Post-Incident Tooling Improvements
After every incident, the postmortem should include a section on tooling effectiveness. Questions to answer:
Was there any tool that responders couldn't access when needed? Why? What needs to change?
Did the runbook link in the alert point to the right document and section?
Was the war room doc created before the bridge call opened? If not, why?
Was any diagnostic information missing from the observability stack that would have shortened the investigation?
Did the war room channel get noisy in a way that slowed down coordination? What discipline broke down?
These questions produce concrete improvement actions. A team that reviews its tooling effectiveness after every incident compounds its response quality over time.
Runbook Integration in the War Room

Runbooks are only useful if the on-call team can find and navigate them under pressure. The war room tooling stack must provide instant runbook access from the incident channel—either through a Slack bot command or through a pinned link in the incident summary. The following Slack bot command pattern integrates runbook retrieval directly into the incident channel.

"""runbook_bot.py -- Slack bot for runbook retrieval during incidents.

Usage in Slack:
  /runbook capability hallucination
  /runbook infra rate-limit
  /runbook security prompt-injection
"""
import os
from slack_bolt import App
from slack_bolt.adapter.aws_lambda import SlackRequestHandler
import httpx

app = App(
    token=os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"],
    signing_secret=os.environ["SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET"],
)

RUNBOOK_INDEX_URL = os.environ["RUNBOOK_INDEX_URL"]  # points to runbook-index.yaml


@app.command("/runbook")
def handle_runbook(ack, respond, command):
    ack()
    parts = command["text"].strip().split()
    if len(parts) < 1:
        respond("Usage: /runbook <category> [type]")
        return

    category = parts[0].lower()
    inc_type = parts[1].lower() if len(parts) > 1 else None

    # Fetch the runbook index
    resp = httpx.get(RUNBOOK_INDEX_URL, timeout=5)
    if resp.status_code != 200:
        respond(f"Could not fetch runbook index: HTTP {resp.status_code}")
        return

    import yaml
    index = yaml.safe_load(resp.text)["runbooks"]

    if category not in index:
        respond(f"Unknown category: {category}. Available: {list(index.keys())}")
        return

    cat_runbooks = index[category]
    if inc_type and inc_type in cat_runbooks:
        entry = cat_runbooks[inc_type]
        respond(
            f":book: *Runbook: {category}/{inc_type}*\n"
            f"URL: {entry['url']}\n"
            f"Summary: {entry['summary']}"
        )
    else:
        # List all runbooks in category
        lines = [f":books: *Runbooks for category: {category}*"]
        for name, entry in cat_runbooks.items():
            lines.append(f"• `{name}`: {entry['summary']} → {entry['url']}")
        respond("\n".join(lines))


def lambda_handler(event, context):
    slack_handler = SlackRequestHandler(app=app)
    return slack_handler.handle(event, context)
War Room Access Audit Automation

Quarterly audits must verify that all IC roster members have current access to every required tool. The following script queries your identity provider and tool APIs to produce a gap report without manual data collection.
Key Takeaways

Agentic AI incidents require dedicated observability tooling—trace viewers with agent step visualization and eval score dashboards—that conventional APM tools do not provide. These must be in the war room stack before any incident occurs.

All tool access must be pre-provisioned. Permission requests during an incident are unacceptable delays. Conduct quarterly war room access audits to verify every IC and Technical Lead candidate has required access.
Standardize the Slack channel naming scheme and post a pinned incident summary within the first 5 minutes. This creates a searchable audit trail without relying on the scribe's memory.
Automate war room setup so the IC arrives to a prepared channel, Jira ticket, and status page component. Manual setup during an incident is delay that comes directly out of resolution time.
The bridge call is a coordination channel, not a debugging channel. Sidebar technical conversations belong in Slack threads, not on the bridge call, so the IC can maintain situational awareness.
